feed.title: blank
feed.subtitle: A simple, whitespace theme for academics. Based on [*folio](https://github.com/bogoli/-folio) design.

entry.title: Complyguard
entry.content:
I’ve always been drawn to work that feels meaningful, work that contributes, to building a safer and more responsible world. That’s what made ComplyGuard’s mission resonate with me from the start.

ComplyGuard is a comprehensive B2G / B2C SaaS platform designed to support regulatory agencies in the gambling industry. It enables centralized monitoring of gaming operations, helping regulators streamline compliance, enforce legal standards, and gain real-time visibility through technology-driven systems.

Within this space, I work as a Data Analyst and Analytics Engineer, operating at the intersection of data, regulation, and real-world impact. My work spans across operational and revenue monitoring, tax compliance, responsible gaming and player behavior, KYC and anti-money laundering, and licensing. I also focus on automating complex reporting processes, data validation, and regulatory workflows to improve efficiency, consistency, and reliability.

I take ownership of the data pipeline from data extraction through to the final analytical layer. This involves transforming and modeling data into structured, reliable data that is ready for reporting, analysis, and decision-making. I build these systems with a focus on scalability, clarity, and long-term maintainability.

A key part of my role is understanding stakeholder needs and translating them into data models that reflect how businesses in the industry actually operate. I also ensure data quality through testing, validation, and clear documentation, so the outputs can be trusted and consistently used.

I often act as a bridge between technical teams and business users, ensuring that data is not only accurate, but also clear, accessible, and meaningful in practice.

At its core, my work is about bringing structure to complexity, turning raw, fragmented data into something dependable and genuinely useful. It’s about solving real challenges within the industry in a way that is efficient, practical, and grounded in its realities.

More information at Complyguard
entry.summary: and my role as a Data and Business Intelligence Analyst

entry.title: The Coalesce Group
entry.content:
One thing about me is that I really enjoy expanding my experience. Whether it’s expanding the industries I work with, the types of data I work with, or different branches of data management, I’m open to all possibilities.

This time, I worked as a Client Reporting Analyst for The Coalesce Group, a B2B sales and lead generation company operating across the UK. I came in to support their weekly campaign reporting, which at the time was still being done manually while they were in the process of migrating to a more automated system.

I worked on weekly reporting for their campaigns, focusing on understanding key sales and funnel metrics, as well as reporting, validating, and ensuring figures were accurate for external stakeholders week to week. A big part of my role was making sure the reports were easy to understand, to the point, and included all the information and recommendations needed for decision-making.

Since the data was scattered across multiple sources, I spent time collecting and bringing it together, then calculating the key KPIs and metrics needed to support weekly decision-making. Although this was a short-term role driven by immediate business needs, it taught me a lot about the industry and the realities of week-to-week reporting, as well as how important it is to not just report data, but also provide context and recommendations that support decisions week to week
entry.summary: and my role as a Client Reporting Analyst

entry.title: Green Bell Packaging
entry.content:
I’ve always loved small businesses. When I went to the UK for university, I ended up supporting loads of them, and that’s actually what inspired my thesis. My main focus was on helping SMEs better leverage technology that is often underutilised, typically due to constraints such as limited budgets, resource availability, and the fact that operational priorities often take precedence over structured data management.

During this period, I worked as a data analyst at Green Bell Packing, a B2B company specialising in sustainable packaging solutions for retail clients across the UK. I was responsible for delivering the end-to-end analytics workflow, developing a business intelligence solution that consolidated operational data into a usable decision-support system.

The company was using Odoo as its ERP/CRM system. I extracted and transformed this data, integrating it into Power BI to create a centralised reporting framework designed to improve visibility across key business functions and support daily operational decision-making.

I collaborated closely with stakeholders across sales, operations, and procurement to translate business requirements into structured analytics outputs, ensuring the dashboards were aligned with practical workflows rather than purely descriptive reporting.

The solution was organised into seven functional reporting areas:

1. Supplier Performance Analysis
This section evaluated supplier reliability and efficiency, including on-time delivery performance, average lead times, order fulfilment status, and supplier-level comparison metrics to identify high and low-performing vendors.

2. Purchase Order Analysis
Focused on procurement activity, this tab provided visibility into total purchase order values, order completion rates, currency distribution, and supplier-wise purchasing behaviour to support cost tracking and procurement planning.

3. Inventory Insights
This module tracked stock availability and movement, including current stock levels, free-to-use versus allocated inventory, product demand trends, and identification of fast- and slow-moving items.

4. Returns and Stock Movement Analysis
Designed to monitor stock adjustments and returns, this section analysed return frequency, reasons where available, product-level return patterns, and overall inventory movement trends over time.

5. Sales Lead Performance
This tab focused on the early sales pipeline, tracking lead generation volumes, lead sources (where available), conversion ratios, and win/loss analysis to assess pipeline effectiveness.

6. Quotation and Sales Conversion Analysis
This area examined the transition from quotation to closed sales, measuring quote conversion rates, customer engagement patterns, and drop-off points within the sales funnel.

7. Sales Order Fulfilment Analysis
This final module tracked order execution performance, including fulfilment status, delivery completion rates, delays, and overall order processing efficiency.

In addition to dashboard development, I also acted in a consultative capacity, identifying inconsistencies within the ERP data structure and highlighting areas where system capabilities were underutilised. This included addressing data quality issues such as inconsistent categorisation, duplication across fields, and gaps in reporting logic, with the aim of improving overall data integrity and usability across the organisation.

Please find the abstract of my thesis at:
entry.summary: and my role as a Data Analyst
